title: Check yourself
questions.0.q: const cups = 12; then a line cups * 2; and finally console.log(cups); What comes out in the console?
questions.0.options.3: Nothing, because the middle line has no console.log
questions.0.hint: The middle line does run. Ask what happens to its result, and whether it touches cups at all.
questions.0.explain: The sum is worked out, but nobody asked for the result to be shown, so it is thrown away at once. And more importantly: `cups * 2` does not change `cups`. It reads the value and produces a new number. To change a name you need an assignment. 12 comes out.
questions.1.q: Which of these lines prints number?
questions.1.hint: Three of the four have quotes somewhere. Look at exactly where they sit and what they wrap.
questions.1.explain: Only the first. The second is a string and gives string, even though 1247 and "1247" look identical on screen — which is exactly what makes the mistake tricky. `typeof null` gives object: a thirty-year-old mistake in the language that cannot be fixed. The fourth is text that happens to read number, and gives string.
questions.2.q: let sold = 1247; let Sold = 1300; How many names does this code create?
questions.2.options.0: One. The second overwrites the first with 1300.
questions.2.options.1: Two separate names, each with its own value.
questions.2.options.2: The code breaks — the name is already taken.
questions.2.options.3: Two, but the second one gives a warning.
questions.2.hint: To the computer letters are characters, not words. Does it tell `s` from `S` the way it tells `a` from `b`?
questions.2.explain: Capitals and lowercase are different characters. Two separate names, zero warnings, zero errors. That is where the quietest mistake in this section comes from: the sum below uses `Sold`, you think it uses `sold`, and the number simply comes out different.
questions.3.q: Which of these lines does NOT break?
questions.3.hint: `const` forbids one particular thing. Ask which of the four lines is not trying to do it.
questions.3.explain: Only the fourth. Reading a constant is allowed — what is forbidden is making the name point at something else. The first reassigns it and gives a TypeError. The second declares it with no value, which is a SyntaxError. The third declares one name twice. Notice that `price + 1` does not change `price` — it produces a new number.
questions.4.q: var total = 100; then a block with curly braces, and inside it var total = 0; After the block, console.log(total) shows:
questions.4.options.0: 100, because the block does not affect the outside
questions.4.options.3: The code breaks — the name is declared twice
questions.4.hint: Does `var` stop at the curly brace? And if it does not — what does a second declaration with the same name do?
questions.4.explain: `var` does not stop at the block. The second declaration does not create a new name — it is ignored, but the assignment still runs and overwrites the outer one. 0 comes out. The same code with `let` gives 100, because the inner name is separate and lives only between the braces.
questions.5.q: A form field returns the number of cups. The code is: const cups = form.value; console.log(cups + 1). The user typed 5. What comes out?
questions.5.options.3: An error, because text is added to a number
questions.5.hint: What does a form field return — a number or text? And what does the plus do when one side is text?
questions.5.explain: Form fields always return text. `"5" + 1` is not a sum — the plus joins and gives `"51"`. Nothing breaks, and that is exactly what makes it dangerous. Only the plus behaves this way: `"5" * 1` gives 5, because multiplication has no second job.
questions.6.q: let a = 5; let b = 9; a = b; b = a; What does console.log(a, b) show?
questions.6.hint: Run the two lines slowly, one at a time. What is `a` holding when the second line starts?
questions.6.explain: The first line makes `a` point at 9 and the five disappears — nothing is holding it any more. The second line takes the NEW value of `a`, which is 9 again. Assignment goes one way and eats the old value. A swap needs a third name to hold the value that is about to vanish.
questions.7.q: The console is COMPLETELY empty and there is one red line in it. Which kind of error is this most likely?
questions.7.options.3: You cannot tell from an empty console
questions.7.hint: If the code had started and stopped halfway, what would be on screen above the red line?
questions.7.explain: A `SyntaxError` is caught while the file is being READ, before a single line has run — that is why the console stays completely empty. The other two stop execution on their own line, and everything printed before them stays on screen. An empty console and a half-full one mean different things, and that is the first thing you look at.
